title: Viburnum tiliifolium
intro: Viburnum tiliifolium, also known as Maple-leaved Viburnum, is a deciduous shrub native to the mountains of Europe and Western Asia, characterized by its maple-like leaves and showy white flowers. The plant has been traditionally used in herbal medicine to treat a range of health issues, including fever, digestive problems, and respiratory infections, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Viburnum tiliifolium include iridoids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal effects. In herbal medicine, Viburnum tiliifolium is often prepared as a tea, tincture, or dried leaf infusion, and is also used in combination with other herbs to create remedies for colds, flu, and other infections.
taxonomy: Viburnum tiliifolium is classified within the family Adoxaceae. It belongs to the genus Viburnum, a group of flowering plants known for their ornamental value. The species Viburnum tiliifolium is further categorized as a perennial shrub native to certain regions. This classification is based on its botanical characteristics and growth habits. Within the genus Viburnum, Viburnum tiliifolium is one of several species that share similar traits.

side_effects: Viburnum tiliifolium is native to eastern Asia and is not commonly associated with significant health risks. However, when ingested, its leaves and berries may cause stomach upset and nausea in some individuals. The plant's extracts are sometimes used in traditional medicine, which may lead to allergic reactions or interactions with other medications in rare cases. Ingesting the plant's berries can also cause vomiting or diarrhea due to their high concentration of saponins. Generally, Viburnum tiliifolium is considered safe when handled properly and in moderation.
